title: practical english usage - Part 67
content:
Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs          7 grammar •  77  weaker obligation: had better You'd better help me. If you don't, there'll be trouble. (order/threat) Could you help me, if you've got time? (request) Had better is not used in polite requests. Compare: soon - he's leaving for South Africa at the end of the month.' 7 really ought to go and see Fred one of these days.' 'Well, you'd better do it ought. Compare: Had better refers to the immediate future. It is more urgent than should or It's seven o'clock. I'd better put the meat in the oven.

You'd better turn that music down before your dad gets angry.

ourselves).

We   use had better to give strong advice, or to tell people what to do (including 1  meaning 77 weake    r  obligation:       had     better didn't ought to do that), but this structure is not used in standard English.

In some English dialects, questions and negatives are made with did (e.g. She He ought to be here soon, shouldn't he?

rare; should is generally used instead.

In American English, interrogative and contracted negative forms of ought to are You ought always to count your change... (more formal; rare in AmE) You always ought to count your change when you buy things.

ought is more common in an informal style.

Mid-position adverbials (►  200) can go before or after ought. The  position before not We ought to wake Ella, oughtn't we? (     .. .oughtn't we to?) To is not used in question tags.

You ought to see a dentist.

different from other modal auxiliary verbs.) After ought, we  use the infinitive with to before other verbs. (This makes ought 3 ought:      forms Alice ought to have spoken to James, but I'm not sure she did.

I should have phoned Ed this morning but I forgot.

done.

past obligations: things which  weren't done, or which  may or may not have been But should/ought to have + past participle can be used to talk about unfulfilled not (     I should clean ...) It was going to be a long day. I was supposed to clean the whole house.

we  can use, for example, was/were supposed to (►  78).

Should and ought to are not normally used to talk about past obligation. Instead, 2 past    use:  should        have      . . .

grammar •  79  willingness: will, can not 'There's the doorbell.' 'I'll go.' (     .. .-L-go?) not I think I'll go to bed. (     I think Igo to bed.) Note that the simple present is not normally used to announce decisions.

'Remember to phone Joe, won't you? 'Yes, I will.' not 'The phone's ringing.' 'I'll answer it', (     frn-going-to-answer-it:) OK. I'll buy the tickets. You can buy supper after the show.

instance if we  are agreeing to do something.

We   often use will when we  tell people about a decision as we  make it, for 1  announcing         decisions:      I'll answer        it.

79_willingness:       will,    can Note the pronunciation: /sə'pəust tə/, not /sə'pəozd  tə/.

This stuff is supposed to kill flies. Let's try it.

He's supposed to be quite rich, you know.

Another use of supposed to is to say what is generally believed.

That's a lovely picture, but what's it supposed to be?

How am I supposed to finish all this work by ten o'clock?

The train's already left. What are we supposed to do now?

Questions with supposed to can suggest that there are problems.

Lucy was supposed to come to lunch. What's happened?

This country is supposed to be a democracy.

There is often a suggestion that things do not happen as planned or expected.

You're not supposed to park on double yellow lines.

We're supposed to pay the Council Tax at the beginning of the month.

Catholics are supposed to go to church on Sundays.

according to the rules or the law, or to say what is (not) expected to happen.

Be supposed + infinitive is used to say what people have to do (or not do) 78 expectations:        sup   po   sed    to You better go now.  I better try again later.

Had ('d) is sometimes dropped in very informal speech.

'He says he won't tell anybody.' 'He'd better not.' 'Shall I put my clothes away? ' You'd better!' Normal unemphatic short answer forms are as follows: Hadn't we better tell him  the truth?
